content.text: When multiple business units share a centralised AI platform, the platform team needs a mechanism to communicate cost to each consumer team fairly and transparently. An internal rate card is the published schedule of prices that the platform charges consumer teams for AI services. It is not the provider's published price — it is a derived internal price that reflects provider cost plus platform overhead, structured to recover the full cost of running the AI programme without creating perverse incentives in consumer team behaviour.

attrs.title: Three-column structure: cost components, markup tiers, published rates

content.text: What an internal rate card prices

The internal rate card prices the AI service at the unit level that consumers understand and can forecast against. The most useful pricing unit is the token — matching the unit the provider charges — but expressed at the model tier that the consumer team uses. This avoids the confusion of a blended rate that averages across models the consumer does not use.
Rate card line item
What it covers
Unit
Input tokens — claude-sonnet-4
Provider input cost + 20% platform overhead
Per 1M tokens
Output tokens — claude-sonnet-4
Provider output cost + 20% overhead
Per 1M tokens
Cache read tokens — claude-sonnet-4
Provider cache cost + 20% overhead
Per 1M tokens
Input tokens — claude-haiku-4
Provider input cost + 20% overhead
Per 1M tokens
Output tokens — claude-haiku-4
Provider output cost + 20% overhead
Per 1M tokens
Platform access fee (optional)
Fixed monthly fee for gateway, logging, monitoring access
Per team per month

Keep the rate card at model level, not at workflow or agent level. Teams need to be able to compute their own bill from first principles given their model usage — a workflow-level rate card creates a black box that cannot be audited by the consumer.
Cost component decomposition

The internal rate is built by stacking provider cost and platform overhead. The FinOps team maintains a cost model that itemises each overhead component so that the markup percentage can be justified to consumer teams and finance.
Provider cost (pass-through)

The provider cost is the actual per-token price from the provider's rate card, adjusted for any committed-use discounts or volume discounts in the current contract. If the organisation has a 25% discount on Claude Sonnet input tokens, the provider cost used in the internal rate card is the discounted price, not the list price.
Platform overhead components

AI gateway cost: hosting cost for the gateway that routes traffic, enforces guardrails, and logs usage. Typically $2,000–$8,000/month depending on traffic volume; allocated across teams proportional to token volume.

Observability stack: cost of storing and querying token usage logs, alert evaluations, and anomaly detection. Typically $500–$2,000/month; allocated equally across active teams or proportional to log volume.

FinOps team time: the fully-loaded cost of the FinOps team's time attributed to AI programme management. Calculate as (FinOps FTE cost × AI programme fraction) / total AI spend; typically adds 8–15% overhead.
Security and compliance tooling: cost of scanning, audit logging, and policy enforcement tools specific to AI. Allocated proportional to API call volume.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RateCardBuilder:
    provider_input_list_price: float     # $/1M tokens (provider list)
    provider_output_list_price: float
    provider_cache_list_price: float
    committed_discount_pct: float        # e.g. 0.25 for 25% discount
    monthly_gateway_cost_usd: float
    monthly_observability_cost_usd: float
    monthly_finops_cost_usd: float
    monthly_other_overhead_usd: float
    monthly_total_tokens_1m: float       # expected total token volume in 1M units

    @property
    def overhead_per_1m_tokens(self) -> float:
        total_overhead = (self.monthly_gateway_cost_usd
                          + self.monthly_observability_cost_usd
                          + self.monthly_finops_cost_usd
                          + self.monthly_other_overhead_usd)
        return total_overhead / max(self.monthly_total_tokens_1m, 1)

    def internal_rate(self, token_type: str) -> float:
        """Compute the internal rate for a given token type."""
        prices = {
            "input": self.provider_input_list_price * (1 - self.committed_discount_pct),
            "output": self.provider_output_list_price * (1 - self.committed_discount_pct),
            "cache_read": self.provider_cache_list_price * (1 - self.committed_discount_pct),
        }
        provider_cost = prices[token_type]
        # Add overhead proportionally (overhead mostly driven by input tokens)
        overhead_share = self.overhead_per_1m_tokens if token_type == "input" else 0
        return provider_cost + overhead_share

builder = RateCardBuilder(
    provider_input_list_price=3.00,
    provider_output_list_price=15.00,
    provider_cache_list_price=0.30,
    committed_discount_pct=0.20,
    monthly_gateway_cost_usd=3_000,
    monthly_observability_cost_usd=800,
    monthly_finops_cost_usd=2_500,
    monthly_other_overhead_usd=700,
    monthly_total_tokens_1m=2_000,     # 2B total tokens / month
)
print(f"Input rate:  ${builder.internal_rate('input'):.3f} / 1M")
print(f"Output rate: ${builder.internal_rate('output'):.3f} / 1M")
Markup tier selection

The markup tier determines how much overhead is built into the internal rate versus recovered through a separate platform fee. The right tier depends on the organisation's chargeback model and its appetite for complexity.
Markup tier
Rate formula
When to use
Zero markup (showback only)
Internal rate = provider cost (no overhead recovery)
Programme is too small to justify overhead allocation; teams are not formally charged
Cost-recovery markup (15–20%)
Internal rate = provider cost × 1.20
Programme has meaningful overhead; teams should see real cost; overhead recovery is the goal
Full chargeback markup (25–35%)
Internal rate = provider cost × 1.30
Platform team operates as a cost centre; full P&L recovery including buffer for investment
Commercial markup (40%+)
Internal rate = provider cost × 1.45+
Platform is a profit centre or external-facing service; must cover margin

Most enterprise AI platform teams operate at the cost-recovery or full chargeback tier. The zero-markup showback tier is appropriate only during the first 3–6 months of a programme while the overhead cost model is being established. The commercial markup tier is appropriate when the AI platform team sells its services to external customers or partner organisations.
Rate card versioning and effective dates

Rate cards must be versioned because provider prices change, committed discounts are renegotiated, and platform overhead grows or shrinks with the programme. Consumer teams need predictability — they cannot forecast their AI costs if the rate card changes without notice.
Effective date rule: announce rate card changes at least 30 days before the effective date. This gives consumer teams time to update their internal forecasts before the new rates apply.
Version numbering: use a simple scheme such as 2026-Q3 (year-quarter). This communicates the intended stability period (one quarter) and makes the version immediately interpretable.
Retroactivity policy: never apply a rate increase retroactively to completed periods. Rate decreases can be applied retroactively at the organisation's discretion.

Rate card archive: maintain a published archive of all prior rate card versions with their effective and end dates. Consumer teams and auditors need to verify the rate that applied to a specific invoice.
Handling currency and multi-region rate cards

Global enterprises using AI models across multiple cloud regions or with multi-currency billing need additional rate card fields. The internal rate card should always express prices in a single reporting currency, with the FX conversion applied at the time of invoice, not at the time of the API call.
Reporting currency: choose USD as the reporting currency even for non-US organisations unless the finance team's P&L is in another currency. Most AI providers bill in USD.
FX conversion: apply the month-end closing rate from the finance team's official FX system to convert regional spend into the reporting currency for the monthly invoice.
Regional multipliers: if infrastructure overhead differs by region (e.g., EU data-residency requirements add cost), maintain per-region overhead multipliers on top of the base rate card.
Publishing and distributing the rate card

The rate card must be discoverable and authoritative. A rate card that exists only in the FinOps team's spreadsheet is not a published rate card — it is a private pricing model. Published means that any team in the organisation can find the current and historical rates without asking the FinOps team.
Internal developer portal: publish the rate card in the same place teams go for API documentation — typically an internal developer portal (Backstage, Confluence, Notion, or a custom wiki).
Machine-readable format: provide a JSON or YAML version of the rate card alongside the human-readable table so teams can programmatically compute their own cost estimates.
Change notification: send a notification to all consumer teams' Slack channels or mailing lists when a new rate card version is published. Do not require teams to check for updates proactively.
Rate calculator: provide a simple web form or spreadsheet calculator where teams can enter expected token volumes and get an estimated monthly cost at the current internal rate.
Rate card reconciliation with actual invoices

The internal rate card is a forward-looking price schedule; the provider invoice is the backward-looking reality. Monthly reconciliation ensures the two align. If the organisation's committed discount was applied correctly, the internal rate card's effective price should closely match the average effective price on the invoice.

A variance above 2% should be investigated. Common causes include: provider applied a one-time credit, committed discount was not applied correctly, usage data in the internal ledger missed some API calls (logging gap), or the provider applied a pricing tier change mid-month that the rate card had not yet reflected.
Consumer team onboarding to the rate card

Consumer teams that are new to AI cost management need guided onboarding to the rate card, not just a link to the documentation. A structured onboarding package accelerates adoption and reduces the number of billing questions the FinOps team handles per month.
Cost estimate worksheet: a pre-populated spreadsheet where teams enter their expected monthly token volumes by model and see a projected monthly cost at internal rates.

Annotated rate card example: a worked example showing how the monthly bill is computed for a representative workflow (e.g., a customer support agent handling 10,000 conversations/month on Claude Sonnet).
FAQ: common questions answered — 'why is the internal rate higher than the provider's published price?', 'how do I get a budget approved for a new workload?', 'what happens if I go over my budget?'
FinOps office hours: monthly 30-minute open session where teams can ask questions about cost management, rate card changes, and optimisation strategies.
Governance: who can change the rate card

The rate card is a financial document — changes to it have P&L implications for every consumer team. Rate card governance should mirror the organisation's financial approval process for other internal pricing decisions.
Proposer: the FinOps team proposes rate card changes based on provider price movements, updated overhead calculations, or commitment discount changes.
Reviewer: the finance partner reviews the proposed rates against the cost model and the overall AI programme P&L.
Approver: the VP Engineering or CFO (depending on the size of the change) formally approves the new rate card before publication.
Communication owner: the FinOps lead sends the change notification to all consumer teams and publishes the new version on the developer portal.
Dispute resolution: consumer teams with objections to a rate card change have 10 business days to raise a formal dispute with the FinOps team; disputes are resolved by the finance partner.
Common mistakes

Using the provider list price as the internal rate without accounting for committed discounts: this causes consumer teams to over-budget and the organisation to 'profit' from AI internally, which distorts investment decisions.
Changing rates mid-period without notice: teams that have already committed spend based on the prior rate cannot retroactively adjust their budgets — always provide 30-day advance notice.

Building a single blended rate across all models: a team running Claude Haiku should not pay the same per-token rate as a team running Claude Opus; per-model rates preserve the price signal that encourages appropriate model selection.
Not versioning the rate card: without version history, auditors cannot verify which rates applied to a specific month's invoice — a compliance risk.
Omitting the overhead decomposition: consumer teams that cannot see how the markup is derived will challenge the rate card as arbitrary; transparency builds trust.
Key takeaways
An internal rate card is built from provider cost plus platform overhead markup — it is not the provider's list price.
Express rates at model level (per 1M input/output/cache tokens per model ID) so consumer teams can compute their own bills from first principles.
Choose a markup tier (showback, cost-recovery, full chargeback, commercial) based on the platform's P&L model and the programme's maturity.
Version rate cards quarterly, announce changes 30 days in advance, and maintain a public archive for audit and dispute resolution.
Publish in machine-readable format alongside the human-readable table and provide a cost calculator so teams can self-serve budget estimates.
